checks.a_analysis: The output text faithfully represents the textual content found in the source PDF. No hallucinated paragraphs or sentences were identified. AI-generated elements like the document title and image alt text are within acceptable parameters.

All numbers, such as currency values, population statistics, and land mass measurements, were cross-referenced and match the source document exactly. No fabricated phone numbers, emails, or URLs are present.
The output contains all major sections and pages from the source. Although specific paragraphs and one table are missing (as noted in C4 and C5), the sections they belong to are present.

The table titled 'NORTH CAROLINA 911 BOARD MEETING ROLL CALL VOTE' on page 10 is entirely absent from the output elements list. Although it appears in the metadata image count, it is not rendered in the JSON content, resulting in a 100% loss of its information (approximately 17 rows).
checks.b_evidence_source: NORTH CAROLINA 911 BOARD MEETING ROLL CALL VOTE table on page 10 containing vote results for board members.
checks.b_evidence_output: The table is missing from the elements array between element 21 ('Roster of Roll Call Votes:') and element 22 ('Adjourn').

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: Two significant omissions were found. First, item 12c ('General Committee Report') on page 9 is missing its final two paragraphs regarding RapidDeploy and Cybersecurity tabletop exercises, which constitutes a 50% loss of that sub-section's text. Second, a brief sentence on page 7 ('Greg Coltrain joined...') is missing between items 10 and 11.
checks.b_evidence_source:
1. 'RapidDeploy is on schedule... moving forward with the project.' and 'The Technology Cybersecurity Ad hoc is planning... how it is integrated.' (Page 9)
2. 'Greg Coltrain joined the meeting at 11:13 AM.' (Page 7)
checks.b_evidence_output:
1. Absent from Section 3, element 18, item 12c.
2. Absent from Section 3, between element 16 and element 17.
checks.b_evidence_location:
1. Section 3, element 18 (Item 12c), Page 9.
2. Section 3, between elements 16 and 17, Page 7.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The wording of the preserved text matches the source document. No meaning-altering differences or transcription errors were found.

checks.a_analysis: The output contains repeated section names like 'General Committee Report' (7c, 8c, 12c) and similar demographic statistics in the NextGen 911 section. However, these repetitions exist in the source document itself (e.g., page 8 repeats the demographic summary at the start and end of section 12c). No blocks of text appear more times in the output than they do in the source.

The heading hierarchy is preserved using section titles. The document title is at the top level, and major minute headers are treated as list items, which is a common structural interpretation for numbered minute items. No levels are skipped in a way that breaks navigation.

The conversion renders the complex tables (Roll Call, Guest List, Roll Call Votes) as images. While this avoids structural corruption of a table object (like merged cells or misaligned rows), it represents a structural simplification. However, given the pipeline's behavior, the relative organization of the tables within the document flow is correct.

On page 10, the text header 'Roster of Roll Call Votes:' precedes the table. In the conversion, the image of the table (Element Index 30) is placed before the text paragraph containing the header (Element Index 31). This inverts the logical flow of the document at that point. Additionally, the headers for the tables on pages 1 and 2 are omitted from the text flow and only appear in the image alt-text.
checks.b_evidence_source: Roster of Roll Call Votes: [Table follows]
checks.b_evidence_output: [Image: Table containing Roster of Roll Call Votes], "content": [{"text": "Roster of Roll Call Votes:"}]

checks.a_analysis: There is a systemic failure in list conversion. Several ordered (lettered) list items that were part of larger sequences (e.g., 5f-j, 8b-c, 10b-c, 12c) have been broken into separate list objects and converted from ordered to 'unordered' list types. This causes the items to lose their structural numbering/lettering identity, even if the characters like 'f)' remain in the text string.
checks.b_evidence_source: f) Cybersecurity... g) NASNA... (part of ordered list 5)

checks.a_analysis: The source document contains a clear hyperlink for 'Live Status Map' in section 5b. The conversion identifies this text and applies formatting (underline), but it fails to include the URI/link metadata required for a functional link.
checks.b_evidence_source: NextGen 911 Migration Status – Live Status Map [Hyperlink]
checks.b_evidence_location: Section 3, Element Index 8, Item b, page 3

checks.a_analysis: The HTML document faithfully preserves the visual organization and logical flow of the source PDF. It presents the meeting title and details, followed by the roll call tables for board members, staff, and guests, and then transitions into the numbered meeting minutes. The final 'Roster of Roll Call Votes' table is correctly placed at the end of the document. The single-column responsive layout is appropriate for HTML and does not confuse the meaning or intent of the original content.

All tables in the document are rendered correctly. The 'Roll Call' tables from PDF pages 1 and 2 and the complex 'Roster of Roll Call Votes' table from page 10 are all displayed with clear cell borders, properly aligned columns, and distinct headers. Despite the source table on page 10 having vertical headers, the HTML correctly renders them horizontally, ensuring the data remains legible and understandable for a sighted user.

The meaningful NC DIT logo image is present and rendered correctly at the start of the document and before the guest roll call table, matching its placement in the source PDF. The red 'APPROVED' watermark from the source is intentionally hidden, which is an acceptable practice for accessibility in HTML conversion.

No content blocks are visually duplicated in the HTML output. The conversion has correctly consolidated the unique information from the 10-page PDF into a single continuous flow without repeating headers, tables, or paragraphs.
